bins.name: State jurisdiction must be preserved
bins.desc: The commissions argue the ANOPR intrudes on exclusive state authority over retail load interconnection, resource adequacy, distribution, and planning. They contend FERC has never before claimed jurisdiction over retail load interconnections and should not do so here, especially without evidence of a problem. Even where technical federal jurisdiction exists, cooperative federalism requires restraint.
quotes: the proposed implementation of that goal raises jurisdictional, reliability, resource adequacy, transparency, planning, and cost allocation concerns

Louisiana and Mississippi have not had any issues associated with the delay of the interconnection of large loads, and FERC should be mindful that any rules adopted could unintentionally result in delays, instead of acceleration of the desired interconnections
The ANOPR may be a solution in search of a problem.
the rule should not apply to any transmission owner or transmission provider in the absence of a demonstration that large load interconnections are being met with significant delays

The ANOPR asserts, without offering any evidence that large load interconnection is unreasonable incumbered, that federal jurisdiction is needed to timely interconnect end-use retail customers, which is traditionally within the purview of retail regulators.

the interconnection process accomplishes little without actions to evaluate and approve any generation, distribution and intrastate transmission facilities needed to maintain reliability and resource adequacy – all matters that are within the exclusive jurisdiction of retail regulators

This expansion of federal jurisdiction is not needed in MISO South and SPP regions, the members of which mainly consist of vertically integrated utilities and cooperatives, and it is not needed in Louisiana and Mississippi.

Even if the FERC technically is granted jurisdiction over large load interconnections to the interstate transmission system, it should refrain from exercising that jurisdiction in locations where it is not needed under principles of cooperative federalism.
Giving FERC jurisdiction over the load interconnection as if it were a standalone issue would hamper the efficient planning and development of these projects.

bins.name: 20 MW threshold too low
bins.desc: The 20 MW threshold would sweep in hundreds of thousands of loads, overwhelming FERC. The commissions urge a much higher threshold limited to loads where interconnection delays have actually been demonstrated.
quotes: The Louisiana and Mississippi Commissions suggest that 20 MW is too low and will result in FERC exercising interconnection jurisdiction over hundreds and thousands of new loads, far beyond FERC's capabilities of reasonably addressing.
The threshold should be far higher and set to only address larger loads that are shown to be affected by interconnection delays.

bins.name: Ratepayer cost protection at risk
bins.desc: States have both the authority and the duty to ensure that the costs of serving large loads do not fall unfairly on retail ratepayers. Federal preemption of load interconnection removes that protective layer.
quotes: the states have the authority and duty to develop protective measures in approving facilities needed to serve large loads, including measures designed to fairly allocate the costs and to protect reliability so that retail ratepayers are not unfairly burdened

bins.name: Cost allocation concerns for retail customers
bins.desc: The filing flags cost allocation as a core concern, warning that federal rules could impose undue costs on retail consumers and undermine state mechanisms that fairly distribute infrastructure costs.
quotes: the proposed implementation of that goal raises jurisdictional, reliability, resource adequacy, transparency, planning, and cost allocation concerns

the states have the authority and duty to develop protective measures in approving facilities needed to serve large loads, including measures designed to fairly allocate the costs and to protect reliability so that retail ratepayers are not unfairly burdened

bins.name: Integrated planning cannot be siloed
bins.desc: The commissions argue that load interconnection cannot be treated as a standalone issue separate from generation, distribution, and transmission planning. A FERC-only interconnection study process disrupts the bottom-up integrated planning that incumbent utilities and states currently perform together.
quotes: the proposed implementation of that goal raises jurisdictional, reliability, resource adequacy, transparency, planning, and cost allocation concerns

the interconnection process accomplishes little without actions to evaluate and approve any generation, distribution and intrastate transmission facilities needed to maintain reliability and resource adequacy – all matters that are within the exclusive jurisdiction of retail regulators
Giving FERC jurisdiction over the load interconnection as if it were a standalone issue would hamper the efficient planning and development of these projects.

bins.name: Regional flexibility required
bins.desc: The commissions support a flexible approach that accommodates regional differences and does not override state processes that are already working. They ask that any rule apply only where delays have been demonstrated and that states retain the ability to manage interconnections under their own successful procedures.
quotes: the rule should not apply to any transmission owner or transmission provider in the absence of a demonstration that large load interconnections are being met with significant delays

Even if the FERC technically is granted jurisdiction over large load interconnections to the interstate transmission system, it should refrain from exercising that jurisdiction in locations where it is not needed under principles of cooperative federalism.

any rules adopted should not be a one-size-fits-all proposition. Rules must take into account regional differences. State and RTO processes that have worked well should be accommodated and not changed. Flexibility is crucial to meeting the needs of new large loads and of the affected states.

bins.name: MISO South process works
bins.desc: Louisiana and Mississippi participate in MISO South through vertically integrated utilities and cooperatives. The commissions say no interconnection delays exist in this region and cite the approval of the $27 billion Meta data center project in under nine months as proof.
quotes: Louisiana and Mississippi have not had any issues associated with the delay of the interconnection of large loads, and FERC should be mindful that any rules adopted could unintentionally result in delays, instead of acceleration of the desired interconnections

This expansion of federal jurisdiction is not needed in MISO South and SPP regions, the members of which mainly consist of vertically integrated utilities and cooperatives, and it is not needed in Louisiana and Mississippi.

any rules adopted should not be a one-size-fits-all proposition. Rules must take into account regional differences. State and RTO processes that have worked well should be accommodated and not changed. Flexibility is crucial to meeting the needs of new large loads and of the affected states.

bins.name: SPP region needs no federal override
bins.desc: The commissions also operate in the SPP footprint and contend the same argument applies: no demonstrated delays, so no federal rule is warranted.
quotes: Louisiana and Mississippi have not had any issues associated with the delay of the interconnection of large loads, and FERC should be mindful that any rules adopted could unintentionally result in delays, instead of acceleration of the desired interconnections

This expansion of federal jurisdiction is not needed in MISO South and SPP regions, the members of which mainly consist of vertically integrated utilities and cooperatives, and it is not needed in Louisiana and Mississippi.

any rules adopted should not be a one-size-fits-all proposition. Rules must take into account regional differences. State and RTO processes that have worked well should be accommodated and not changed. Flexibility is crucial to meeting the needs of new large loads and of the affected states.
